quote: deep learning as we knew it then/as it was mostly commonly used then was greedy, heavily dependent on massive data, and that it would have trouble reasoning
claim: Deep learning is data-greedy, heavily dependent on massive data, and has trouble with reasoning

quote: LLMs were subject to essentially the same critique: greedy with respect to data, uninterpretable, unreliable as reasoners, subject to hallucinations
claim: LLMs are data-greedy, uninterpretable, unreliable as reasoners, and subject to hallucinations

quote: they lacked world models
claim: LLMs lack world models

quote: they don't develop robust representations of *how events unfold over time*
claim: LLMs do not develop robust representations of how events unfold over time

quote: they would be inadequate on their own
claim: LLMs are inadequate as standalone systems for achieving general intelligence

quote: scale was not all we need
claim: Scaling alone is not sufficient for achieving general AI capabilities

quote: the importance of representing kinds distinctly from individuals, separating reasoning from latent representations, incorporating explicit compositional structure and explicit operations over variables, incorporating some degree of innate pre learning, and exploiting explicit knowledge databases
claim: AI systems need to represent kinds distinctly from individuals, separate reasoning from latent representations, incorporate explicit compositional structure and operations over variables, incorporate innate priors, and exploit explicit knowledge databases

quote: DeepMind, among others, has achieved some strong results with neurosymbolic approaches (e.g., AlphaGeometry)
claim: Neurosymbolic approaches have achieved strong results, as demonstrated by DeepMind's AlphaGeometry

quote: models need to innately be more structured than pure multilayer perceptions, even prior to learning
claim: AI models need innate structure beyond pure multilayer perceptrons, prior to learning

quote: GPT-4 is not actually equivalent to a smart high schooler. It can do some things better, some worse, very few reliably.
claim: GPT-4 is not equivalent to a smart high schooler; it performs some tasks better, some worse, and very few reliably

quote: I would not hire it to do most of things that high school students are often hired to do, like work as a lifeguard, work in restaurants, work in a grocery store
claim: GPT-4 could not perform most jobs that high school students are hired to do, such as lifeguarding, restaurant work, or grocery store work

quote: The graph assumes that all continued progress will be same — ignoring qualitatively unsolved problems (hallucinations, planning, reasoning etc), the fact that synthetic data may not be as useful as natural data, limits on then-possible energy distribution and compute
claim: Projections of continued AI progress at past rates ignore qualitatively unsolved problems like hallucinations, planning, and reasoning, the limited utility of synthetic data compared to natural data, and energy and compute limits

quote: GPT-4 existed in August 2022. I can't think of a single measure in which any existing model in 2024 outpace GPT-4 by as much as GPT-4 outpaced the state of the art in 2020. We are already behind the curve.
claim: As of mid-2024, no existing model outpaces GPT-4 by as much as GPT-4 outpaced the 2020 state of the art, indicating AI progress is already behind exponential projections

quote: self-regulation is a farce
claim: Self-regulation by AI companies is ineffective

quote: the US legislature has made almost no progress thus far in reining in Silicon Valley
claim: The US legislature has made almost no progress in regulating Silicon Valley as of mid-2024

trigger: Pitchbook report on GenAI seed funding drop
quote: First sign of GenAI winter?
claim: Declining GenAI seed-stage funding is an early indicator of a potential GenAI winter

trigger: California SB-1047 AI safety bill backlash
quote: We should be making SB-1047 stronger, not weaker.
claim: California's SB-1047 AI safety bill should be strengthened, not weakened

trigger: California SB-1047 AI safety bill backlash
quote: Fine to talk big in front of the Senate, but when push comes to shove, nobody in the industry seems to actually want the kind of regulation they previously hinted at.
claim: AI companies publicly support regulation but oppose it when actual legislation is proposed

trigger: California SB-1047 AI safety bill backlash
quote: Big tech seemingly will not be content until they have squeezed out every last cent of profit, whilst socializing the costs of every last risk.
claim: Big tech companies seek to maximize profits while externalizing AI risks to society

trigger: California SB-1047 AI safety bill backlash
quote: An unregulated AI industry is a recipe for disaster.
claim: An unregulated AI industry will lead to disaster

quote: the database that indexes the whole thing is in plain text (except on systems that are explicitly set to encrypt everything). That's bananas. Keystone Kops level security.
claim: Microsoft Recall's plain-text database demonstrates that tech companies cannot be trusted to self-regulate on security and privacy

trigger: California SB-1047 AI safety bill backlash
quote: AI companies should be subject to liability and basic safety requirements, just as companies in other industries are
claim: AI companies should be subject to liability and basic safety requirements comparable to other industries

quote: As of today, I am more confident than ever that GPT-5 won't land this year.
claim: GPT-5 will not be released in 2024

quote: GPT-5 will continue to hallucinate and make a bunch of wacky errors, whenever it finally drops
claim: GPT-5, whenever released, will still hallucinate and make significant errors

quote: with GPT 4-Turbo and 4o perhaps being failed attempts that didn't seem worthy of the name, with another earlier attempt halting before Sam's time speaking to the Senate in May 2023
claim: GPT-4 Turbo and GPT-4o may have been failed attempts at GPT-5 that didn't meet the bar for the GPT-5 name

quote: stands by his own prediction that we will not see AGI by 2029
claim: AGI will not be achieved by 2029

quote: LLMs are not AGI, and (on their own) never will be; scaling alone was never going to be enough.
claim: LLMs are not AGI and will never achieve AGI on their own; scaling alone is insufficient

quote: The only mystery was what would happen when the big players realized that the jig was up, and that scaling was not in fact "All You Need".
claim: Major AI companies are beginning to realize that scaling LLMs is insufficient for achieving AGI

quote: inside the labs we have these capable models and they're not that far ahead
claim: OpenAI's CTO admitted there is no mind-blowing GPT-5 behind the scenes as of mid-2024

quote: Expect more revisionism and downsized expectations throughout 2024 and 2025.
claim: AI industry leaders will increasingly revise their timelines downward and reduce expectations throughout 2024 and 2025

quote: Generative AI does in fact (still) have enormous limitations
claim: Generative AI still has enormous limitations as of mid-2024

quote: It still hallucinates, it still makes boneheaded mistakes, and the whole Generative AI thing is looking more and more like a dud
claim: Generative AI continues to hallucinate and make significant errors, and is increasingly looking like a commercial dud

quote: OpenAI may in fact yet turn out to be the WeWork of AI
claim: OpenAI may turn out to be the WeWork of AI -- a massively overvalued company that fails to deliver

quote: Scaling is not enough. Returns are starting to diminish. We do need new approaches to AI.
claim: Returns from scaling AI are diminishing and new approaches to AI are needed

quote: AI is going to change everything, if not tomorrow, sometime over the next 5-20 years, some ways for good, some for bad
claim: AI will change everything over the next 5-20 years, in both positive and negative ways

quote: The markers that our next President puts down—about how much liability and responsibility companies will have, and about how the government keeps up with a rapidly changing technology— will have lasting impact.
claim: US presidential AI policy decisions in the near term will have lasting impact on liability, responsibility, and government capacity to keep up with AI

quote: Altman, for example, promised a week ago at Aspen Ideas that AI would "discover all of physics", a claim that is neither coherent nor plausible anytime in the next couple decades
claim: The claim that AI will discover all of physics is neither coherent nor plausible within the next couple of decades

quote: We need a President who can recognize when corporate leaders are promising things far beyond is currently realistic, who can sort truth from bullshit, in order to develop AI policies that are grounded in reality.
claim: The US needs a president who can distinguish AI hype from reality and develop grounded AI policy

quote: we need a President who can get Congress (which has this far dithered) to recognize the true urgency of the moment, since Executive Orders alone are not enough
claim: Executive orders alone are insufficient for AI governance; Congressional legislation is needed

trigger: Bill Gates clip on AI scaling
quote: so many folks have been brainwashed by the "scale is all you need" notion and "AGI in 2025" (or 2027 or 2029) nonsense, none of which seems remotely plausible to me as a cognitive scientist
claim: The notion that scaling alone will achieve AGI by 2025, 2027, or 2029 is not remotely plausible

trigger: Bill Gates clip on AI scaling
quote: many tech leaders have discovered that the best way to raise valuations is to hint that AGI is imminent, always on the dubious premise that scaling will keep going indefinitely; it behooves them financially to pretend
claim: Tech leaders hint at imminent AGI to raise valuations, driven by financial incentives rather than technical reality

trigger: Bill Gates clip on AI scaling
quote: we need techniques, such as metacognition, viz. systems that can reflect on what is needed and how to achieve it, "understanding how to think about a problem in a broad sense and be able to step back" and reflect
claim: AI progress requires metacognition -- systems that can reflect on problems and evaluate their own reasoning approaches

trigger: Bill Gates clip on AI scaling
quote: Spending upwards of 100 billion dollars on the current approach, which is what two more iterations of scaling would likely require, given that is unlikely to get to AGI (or ever be reliable) seems wasteful to me
claim: Spending $100+ billion on further LLM scaling is wasteful because it is unlikely to achieve AGI or reliable AI

trigger: Bill Gates clip on AI scaling
quote: I don't think metacognition can work without bringing explicit symbols (the basis of classical AI) back into the mix
claim: Metacognition in AI requires explicit symbolic representations and cannot work with neural networks alone

trigger: Bill Gates clip on AI scaling
quote: Neurosymbolic AI has long been an underdog; in the end I expect it to come from behind and be essential.
claim: Neurosymbolic AI will eventually prove essential to achieving advanced AI

quote: If a single bug can take down airlines, banks, retailers, media outlets, and more, what on earth makes you think we are ready for AGI?
claim: The fragility of current software infrastructure, as demonstrated by the CrowdStrike outage, shows the world is not ready for AGI

quote: The world needs to up its software game massively. We need to invest in improving software reliability and methodology, not rushing out half-baked chatbots.
claim: The world needs to invest in improving software reliability and methodology rather than rushing out AI chatbots

quote: part of the reason we are struggling with AI in complex AI systems is that we still lack adequate techniques for engineering complex systems
claim: AI struggles are partly due to the field still lacking adequate techniques for engineering complex software systems

quote: Chasing black box AI, difficult to interpret, and difficult to debug, is not the answer.
claim: Pursuing black-box AI that is difficult to interpret and debug is not the path to reliable AI

quote: leaving more and more code writing to generative AI, which grasps syntax but not meaning, as a new benchmark from Stephen Wolfram shows, is not the answer, either
claim: Generative AI grasps code syntax but not meaning, making it inadequate as a replacement for human code writing

quote: An unregulated AI industry is a recipe for disaster.
claim: An unregulated AI industry will lead to disaster

quote: Long-term adoption (vs mere experimentation) has been modest, there is no significantly better tech now than there was in January; GPT-5 is still a no show.
claim: Long-term enterprise adoption of GenAI has been modest rather than transformative, with no significant technical improvement from January to July 2024

quote: LLMs have just became exactly the commodity I predicted they would become, at the lowest possible price. How can OpenAI survive in that context?
claim: LLMs have become commoditized, with Meta offering GPT-4-level models for free, undermining OpenAI's business model

quote: OpenAI, as far as I can tell, doesn't really have any moat whatsoever, beyond brand recognition
claim: OpenAI has no meaningful competitive moat beyond brand recognition

quote: OpenAI could wind up being seen as the WeWork of AI
claim: OpenAI could end up being seen as the WeWork of AI due to huge losses, no moat, and commoditized technology

quote: The neural network approach known as generative AI — especially its poster chatbot ChatGPT – has captured the world's imagination, but will soon flame out.
claim: Generative AI will soon flame out in terms of public enthusiasm and commercial viability

quote: I fully expect that the generative AI bubble will begin to burst within the next 12 months
claim: The generative AI financial bubble will begin to burst within 12 months of July 2024

quote: The current approach has reached a plateau
claim: LLM capabilities have reached a plateau as of mid-2024

quote: There is no killer app
claim: There is no killer app for generative AI

quote: Nobody has a moat
claim: No GenAI company has a meaningful competitive moat

quote: I am certain that the impending collapse won't lead to the absolute death of AI. There is too much at stake.
claim: The collapse of the GenAI bubble will not lead to the death of AI as a field

quote: its collapse may well lead to an AI winter of sorts, like what happened in the mid-1980s
claim: The collapse of GenAI may lead to an AI winter similar to the mid-1980s expert systems bust

quote: What the collapse of generative AI might lead to, after a quiet period, is a renaissance.
claim: After a quiet period following the GenAI collapse, a renaissance in AI using better techniques will emerge

quote: The biggest intrinsic failings of generative AI have to do with reliability, in a way that I believe can never be solved, given their inherent nature.
claim: The reliability problems of generative AI are inherent to the approach and can never be fully solved

quote: At some level, generative AI is a bit like (though not exactly like) a lookup table
claim: Generative AI functions approximately like a lookup table, good at retrieving trained-on patterns but poor at generalizing beyond them

quote: they are better at things that they have encountered before than things that they haven't. They are especially poor if new items are different in important and subtle ways from old ones.
claim: LLMs perform better on inputs similar to their training data and are especially poor when inputs differ in subtle but important ways

quote: LLMs are simply never going to work reliably, at least not in the general form that so many people last year seemed to be hoping
claim: LLMs will never work reliably as general-purpose systems

quote: the deepest problem is that LLMs literally can't sanity-check their own work
claim: LLMs cannot sanity-check their own work, which is their deepest structural limitation

quote: they are simply and true next-word predictors (or, as I once put it, "autocomplete on steroids"), with no inherent way of verifying whether their predictions are correct
claim: LLMs are next-word predictors with no inherent mechanism for verifying the correctness of their outputs

quote: any "reasoning" or "planning" in LLMs is hit or miss, viable if the particulars of a given situation are close enough to what is in the training set, but highly vulnerable if they are not
claim: LLM reasoning and planning is unreliable: it works when situations resemble training data but fails when they do not

quote: Hallucinations and boneheaded errors of reasoning and what I like to call discomprehensions are, as far as I can tell, an inevitable byproduct of LLMs
claim: Hallucinations, reasoning errors, and discomprehensions are an inevitable byproduct of the LLM architecture

quote: Since LLMs inevitably hallucinate and are constitutionally incapable of checking their own work, there are really only two possibilities: we abandon them, or we use them as components in larger systems that can reason and plan better
claim: LLMs must either be abandoned or used as components in larger neurosymbolic systems that can reason and plan

quote: two of the most dominant (and in my humble opinion most misguided) figures in AI, Geoffrey Hinton and Yann LeCun have, for various reasons never fully explained, fought this potential approach
claim: Hinton and LeCun have been misguided in opposing neurosymbolic approaches to AI

quote: OpenAI has for the most part stuck to Hinton and LeCun's dogma, focusing the far greater part of its efforts on "scaling" LLMs (making them bigger and bigger with more data), and trying hard to eschew symbols and symbolic rules
claim: OpenAI has dogmatically focused on scaling LLMs while eschewing symbolic reasoning approaches

quote: all the major tech companies are building exactly the same thing (bigger and bigger LLMs based on more data), achieving almost exactly the same results (a passel of GPT-4 level models, hardly different from each other, all struggling with hallucinations and boneheaded errors), and hardly any investment in anything else
claim: Major AI companies are all building nearly identical LLMs with nearly identical results and nearly identical flaws, with minimal investment in alternative approaches

quote: GDM's new work is powerful proof of concept (pun intended!). Neural networks and symbol systems can be profitably combined, contra Hinton and LeCun.
claim: Google DeepMind's AlphaProof and AlphaGeometry results prove that neural networks and symbolic systems can be profitably combined

quote: neurosymbolic approaches themselves aren't a panacea or magic bullet—much more is required, including an infrastructure of knowledge a way of deriving cognitive models from inputs such as text and video
claim: Neurosymbolic approaches are necessary but not sufficient; they also require knowledge infrastructure and cognitive model derivation from inputs

quote: expecting AI to "solve" AGI without "System II" mechanisms for symbol-manipulation is like expecting bears to solve quantum mechanics. There can be no path to AGI without neurosymbolic AI.
claim: There is no path to AGI without neurosymbolic AI incorporating System II symbolic reasoning

quote: Many coders and tech aficionados may love ChatGPT for work, but much of the outside world feels quite differently.
claim: GenAI is popular among coders and tech enthusiasts but most non-technical workers find it unhelpful or counterproductive

trigger: Multiple negative GenAI news reports in one week
quote: I have long argued that Generative AI might be a dud; I just didn't expect it to fade so fast.
claim: Generative AI is fading commercially faster than expected

trigger: Multiple negative GenAI news reports in one week
quote: The bubble has begun to burst. Users have lost faith, clients have lost faith, VC's have lost faith.
claim: The GenAI bubble has begun to burst as of late July 2024, with users, clients, and VCs losing faith

trigger: Multiple negative GenAI news reports in one week
quote: The fact that the GenAI bubble is apparently bursting sooner than expected may soon free up resources for other approaches, e.g., into neurosymbolic AI
claim: The bursting of the GenAI bubble may free up resources for alternative approaches like neurosymbolic AI

quote: current approaches to machine learning (which underlies most of the AI people talk about today) are lousy at outliers
claim: Current machine learning approaches are fundamentally poor at handling outliers -- inputs that differ significantly from training data

quote: Machine learning had trouble with outliers then, and it still does.
claim: Machine learning's inability to handle outliers has persisted for over 30 years and remains unsolved

quote: An entire industry has been built - and will collapse - because people aren't getting it.
claim: The GenAI industry will collapse because most participants do not understand that current ML is fundamentally poor at outliers

quote: The people who have temporarily gotten rich or famous on AI have done so by pretending that this outlier problem simply doesn't exist, or that a remedy for it is imminent.
claim: AI industry leaders have gotten rich by pretending the outlier problem does not exist or that a fix is imminent

quote: The "localism" in how neural networks are trained makes that an inherent problem.
claim: The local nature of neural network training makes poor outlier generalization an inherent and unsolvable problem within the neural network paradigm

quote: Neural networks don't have a good way of acquiring sufficiently general abstractions.
claim: Neural networks lack the ability to acquire sufficiently general abstractions needed for reliable performance on novel inputs

quote: Symbolic systems have always been good for outliers; neural networks have always struggled with them.
claim: Symbolic systems handle outliers well while neural networks struggle with them, making hybrid approaches necessary

quote: You can't think we are close to AGI once you realize that as yet we have no general solution to the outlier problem. You just can't.
claim: AGI is not close because there is no general solution to the outlier problem in neural networks

quote: everything that people like Altman and Musk and Kurzweil are currently saying about AGI being nigh seems like sheer fantasy, on par with imagining that really tall ladders will soon make it to the moon
claim: Claims by Altman, Musk, and Kurzweil that AGI is imminent are sheer fantasy given the unsolved outlier problem

quote: Generative AI, at least we know it know, doesn't actually work that well, and maybe never will.
claim: Generative AI does not work well and may never work well

quote: the collapse of the generative AI bubble – in a financial sense – appears imminent, likely before the end of the calendar year
claim: The GenAI financial bubble will likely collapse before the end of 2024

quote: investors may well stop forking out money at the rates they have, enthusiasm may diminish, and a lot of people may lose their shirts
claim: GenAI investment rates will slow, enthusiasm will diminish, and many investors will lose significant money

quote: Companies that are currently valued at billions of dollars may fold, or stripped for parts. Few of last year's darlings will ever meet recent expectations
claim: Some billion-dollar GenAI companies will fold or be stripped for parts, and few will meet their recent valuation expectations

quote: Things may look radically different by the end of 2024 from how they looked just a few months ago.
claim: The GenAI industry landscape will look radically different by the end of 2024 compared to early 2024

trigger: Nvidia stock drop, OpenAI GPT-5 delay, Elon suing OpenAI, Google antitrust loss
quote: LLMs are not AGI, and (on their own) never will be; scaling alone was never going to be enough. The only mystery was what would happen when the big players realized that the jig was up, and that scaling was not in fact "All You Need".
claim: LLMs will never be AGI on their own and scaling alone was never going to be sufficient

trigger: Nvidia stock drop, OpenAI GPT-5 delay, Elon suing OpenAI, Google antitrust loss
quote: it is also not out of the question that today could end someday end up being seen as a turning point
claim: August 5, 2024 could be seen as a turning point in the deflation of the GenAI bubble

quote: OpenAI could wind up being seen as the WeWork of AI. Huge operating losses, big staff expenses, an apparent morale problem, no GPT-5 yet, competitors catching up, Meta giving away similar tech for free, Microsoft cutting deals with competitors.
claim: OpenAI could become the WeWork of AI due to huge losses, morale problems, no GPT-5, competitors catching up, and free alternatives from Meta

quote: Will they earn enough to justify their $80B valuation? Raise a future round at even higher valuation, when the money runs out? Prospects don't seem as strong as they once did.
claim: OpenAI's prospects for justifying its $80B valuation or raising future rounds are weakening

quote: The board, which basically said it couldn't trust Sam, may have had a point.
claim: The OpenAI board that tried to fire Sam Altman over trust issues may have been justified

trigger: OpenAI webcam investment and data collection practices
quote: Altman wants to know - and monetize - everything about you.
claim: Sam Altman is pursuing a strategy to collect and monetize comprehensive personal data about users
